quote: What's your interest in solar? Just saving money?
stewart_read: Bucky leads the answer — 'just saving money?' — converting the schema's most important emotional anchor question into a yes/no checkbox. Cedric confirms ('Yes. And, like, Allstate. You know?') and adds an insurance angle that Bucky doesn't probe. The bill anchor is captured as a data point but never flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. Reactive read holds: this is a missed depth moment, not a strategic deferral — there's no later move that picks it up.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky was moving efficiently because Cedric was already sold and didn't need emotional work. Rejected — the schema's bill_anchoring principle applies regardless of customer warmth; the flip is about arming the closer, not overcoming resistance.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask Bucky what Cedric said after 'just saving money' — he'll likely not remember the Allstate comment. That's the tell: he wasn't listening for depth, he was listening for confirmation. Walk him through the capture-acknowledge-flip sequence from coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring. The flip doesn't require a hostile customer to land.

stewart_read: Bucky notes HOA presence on the record — apparently from the satellite map view — but never asks Cedric whether the HOA has solar restrictions. The rear-placement constraint Cedric volunteers at 07:40 ('it has to be on the rear of the house, it cannot be seen from the street') is a direct HOA rule. Bucky had the signal at 01:37 and walked past it. The constraint was foreseeable, not a surprise. Reactive read holds: this is an early signal missed, not a strategic deferral.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky planned to let the closer handle HOA details at the design stage. Rejected — the constraint affects whether a design is even buildable as proposed; surfacing it at verify protects the sit from collapsing at the closer call.
coaching_implication: When HOA is flagged on the map, the follow-up question is mandatory: 'Does your HOA have any rules about where panels can go?' One question at 01:37 would have surfaced the rear-placement rule before booking, giving the closer a clean brief instead of a surprise constraint.

quote: I can tell you what my kilowatt usage is right now.
stewart_read: Cedric has the Dominion app open and is ready to share live usage data — he's not just cooperative, he's technically engaged and motivated enough to pull the data in real time. This is a qualification-positive signal: a new homeowner who immediately opens his utility app on a sales call is a high-intent lead. Bucky accepts the data but doesn't anchor on the enthusiasm — he treats it as a logistics problem (get the photo) rather than a trust-building moment.
coaching_implication: Surface this as a positive pattern for Bucky to recognize: when a customer self-solves a data problem, that's an enthusiasm signal worth naming aloud. A line like 'You've already got the app — that tells me you're serious about this' costs nothing and deepens the rapport before the hold.

quote: Okay. Let me just go talk to him, and then I think we'll we'll be good to go.
stewart_read: Bucky puts Cedric on hold to get supervisor approval on whether a single current-day kWh reading (182 kWh) is sufficient to proceed. This reveals he doesn't know the internal threshold for what constitutes an approvable data point on a new-homeowner account. The hold adds dead air and mild friction to a call that had been frictionless. Reactive read holds: this is a genuine knowledge gap, not a strategic pause.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky was being diligent by confirming with a specialist before booking. Rejected — a rep who knows the floor's bill-approval criteria doesn't need a hold; the hold is the tell.
coaching_implication: This is a training gap, not a behavior gap. Bucky needs a clear internal rule: what data is sufficient to book when a customer has no prior bill history? Once he has that rule, the hold disappears and the call tightens by 90 seconds. Escalate to floor training if other reps are hitting the same hold pattern on new-homeowner leads.

quote: I'll be at home all day. I got some stuff getting delivered, so I'll be there.
stewart_read: Cedric chose March 27 because he has deliveries that day — he'll be home, but his attention will be split. He then narrows to noon specifically ('If you do it early in the day, that's great. Then I won't be sitting at home all day waiting') to minimize the wait. The appointment is anchored to a delivery day, not to a clear-calendar day. Bucky books noon without flagging the split-attention risk or reinforcing the 'at your computer, not driving' button-up requirement.
coaching_implication: Flag for the closer: Cedric chose this day for logistical convenience, not because it's his best focus day. The closer should open with a quick 'deliveries all sorted?' check-in to gauge attention level before diving into the design. Bucky should have reinforced the computer/no-driving anchor at button-up — its absence here is a no-show risk factor.

quote: I'm gonna get your design built, and then we will see you on the twenty seventh at noon.
stewart_read: Bucky's button-up is missing four of six required elements: the one-hour block statement, the phone-appointment specification, the no-driving/at-computer anchor, and the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. He also makes no mention of a follow-up text. The appointment is confirmed on date and time, but the structural commitments that predict show rate are absent. The call ends 18 seconds later with no recovery of these elements.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky kept it brief because Cedric was already committed and a longer button-up risked re-opening questions. Rejected — the button-up elements are show-rate predictors, not persuasion tools; skipping them on a warm lead is still a protocol gap.
coaching_implication: Walk Bucky through the full button_up checklist against this call. He hit one of six elements. The text follow-up omission is particularly costly here: Cedric is traveling, will be home on a delivery day, and has an HOA constraint the closer needs to know about. A personalized text ('looking forward to reviewing your rear-placement design on the 27th') would have served as both a show-rate anchor and a constraint acknowledgment. Per schema section 9, the tieback is required — it's not optional on warm leads.

quote: I'll also just let you know, it has to be on the rear of the house. It cannot be, seen from the street.
stewart_read: Cedric volunteers the HOA rear-placement rule as the call is closing. Bucky's response ('Okay. That's fine. You just talk to the specialist, and you guys will can figure that out together') is technically correct scope-holding — this is closer-tier content — but the constraint is not documented for the handoff. The closer will walk into this appointment without knowing the design must satisfy a rear-placement restriction. If the closer's first design doesn't account for this, the sit risks collapsing on a constraint that was disclosed on the setter call.
coaching_implication: Bucky's scope-hold was right. The documentation failure is the gap. The handoff brief must carry this constraint explicitly. In the 1-on-1, reinforce: 'You handled the scope correctly — now show me where you wrote that down for the closer.' If there's no CRM field for HOA constraints, that's a floor-level process gap worth flagging to management.

stewart_read: Bucky states the credit threshold as '$6.70' — a verbal artifact of reading '670' as a dollar figure. Cedric confirms without apparent confusion, but the phrasing is garbled enough that it's unclear whether Cedric understood the question as a credit score threshold. More substantively, Bucky asks the question and moves on in one breath ('Yes. Perfect. Alright. Yeah. That's all we need.') — no acknowledgment, no bridge. The schema threshold is 670; the SESSION18_fcbf33c1 drift to 650 is a separate issue, but the garbled delivery here is its own coaching moment.
coaching_implication: This is a delivery issue, not a knowledge issue — Bucky knows the qualifier exists. In the 1-on-1, have him say the question aloud: 'Is your credit score above 670?' Clean delivery matters because a confused customer may confirm without actually qualifying. Low-stakes fix: slow down on the credit question, state it as a score not a dollar amount.
